1:
The book opens its tenth chapter abruptly, on a lost battlefield, and the witnesses read that abruptness as deliberate. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes the curt beginning — comparable to the sudden openings elsewhere in Scripture, as at Isaiah 2:1 — and fixes the scene in the plain of Jezreel, or Esdraelon, that broad theatre of so many of ancient Israel's struggles, where Hosea would later hear God threaten to break the bow of Israel in the valley of Jezreel in Hosea 2:10. He presses the Hebrew: the men of Israel is literally the man of Israel, a collective that makes the rout more vivid — they fled as one body, where the older narrative of 1 Samuel 31 has the plural. Mount Gilboa, the Jebel Fuku'a, rises some seventeen hundred feet out of the plain; the defeated army fell back upon it, the very height that had been their first position in 1 Samuel 28:4, only to be cut down there.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch lift their eyes from the battle to its place in the book. The ruin of Saul and his house, they explain, is set here as the introduction to the whole history of David's kingship: the Chronicler does not retell the reign of Saul but records only his death, because by that death the way was opened for David to the throne. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke agrees on the writer's economy — he lays out the chapter as a fatal battle at Gilboa in which Saul is mortally wounded and three sons slain, then the Philistine triumph over the bodies, the rescue by the men of Jabesh, and the reason for Saul's fall — and reminds the reader that almost the same words stand in 1 Samuel 31, to which he refers for all that is important.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry and the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley draw the moral the bare notice carries. Princes sin, they say, and the people suffer for it; it was a black day when Israel fled before the Philistines and quitted the cities the enemy then occupied at v.7. Henry observes that they were not at this time guilty of the idolatry that stained the days of the judges and later of the kings, for Samuel had reformed them; yet they were given to the spoil, because what divine justice chiefly eyed was the sin of Saul. And as princes sin, so parents sin and children suffer: when the measure of Saul's iniquity was full he not only fell himself but drew his sons down with him, Jonathan the gracious among them, the iniquity of the father visited on the children. Great men, Wesley warns, must above all take heed of provoking God's wrath, for if they kindle that fire they know not how many may be consumed for their sakes. The witnesses do not divide; they open the chapter with one voice, reading a military disaster as a moral reckoning.
2:
The pursuit closes on the king himself, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott dwells on the very verb. Followed hard after Saul, he explains, is literally clave to Saul — the Philistines fastened on him and hotly pursued, as an army presses a fleeing king in 1 Kings 22:31; for the destruction of the king and his sons together would make their triumph complete. He corrects a small point of the English: it should read simply the sons of Saul, without the article, and he observes who is missing from the list. Only three sons fall here — Jonathan, Abinadab, and Malchishua — while Eshbaal, Saul's fourth son, was not in the battle, as we learn from 2 Samuel 2:8 and the genealogy of 1 Chronicles 8:33, and survived to be set up briefly over the northern tribes.

Ellicott lets the human tragedy show through the terse Hebrew. Like Zedekiah, the last king of Judah, who saw his sons slaughtered before his own eyes were put out in 2 Kings 25:7, Saul may well have witnessed the death of his own sons; Jonathan, at the least, would not have been far from his father in that last struggle. Over the whole scene Ellicott sets the ancient elegy's word — in their deaths they were not divided — so that the very completeness of the ruin becomes a kind of terrible fidelity, father and son falling together on the one mountain.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had already found the lesson the list of names teaches: when the measure of Saul's iniquity was full, his sons perished with him, all but the one who was absent, and even Jonathan — that gracious and generous man — fell as part of the condemned house, the iniquity of the father visited upon the children. Those who love their seed, Henry urges, must leave their sins, lest they not only perish in their own guilt but entail ruin upon their families when they are gone. The two witnesses do not contend; the grammarian and the preacher together read in the fall of Saul's sons both the accuracy of the record and the sober justice of God, who spares neither the crown nor the bloodline of a king who has set himself against the word of the LORD.
3:
As the fighting tightens around the wounded king, the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott weighs each word of the Hebrew. The battle went sore against Saul is, more literally, that it was heavy upon him, pressing him down like a burden that weighs a man to the earth. The archers hit him he renders they that shoot with the bow came upon him, and he shuddered before the shooters — the verb being properly to writhe or travail, the word used of anguish in Deuteronomy 2:25 and Isaiah 23:4. Saul's deadly terror, Ellicott insists, was entirely natural: he believed himself forsaken of God, and stood, after a lost battle, beset by murderous foes whom he could not reach, with no chance of a fair hand-to-hand encounter. Death was coming at a distance, on the arrow's point, and he could not close with it.

Ellicott adds a historian's touch about the weapon. The bow was a favourite arm of the Philistines, whose military art owed much to Egypt; the very hieroglyph for a soldier, he notes, is the figure of a man carrying bow and quiver. So the instrument that struck Saul down was the characteristic instrument of the uncircumcised power that had harried Israel throughout his reign, and it found him where valour could not answer it.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry hears in that arrow a stroke of exact retribution. The manner of Saul's fall, he observes, answered in point after point to his sins: he had thrown a javelin more than once at David and missed him, but now the archers hit him and he was wounded of the archers. The man who had turned his own weapon against the LORD's anointed is at last brought down by weapons he cannot parry, the hunter of David caught in the open field. Henry's reading and Ellicott's do not compete but overlay: what the grammarian describes as the natural terror of a forsaken king, the preacher reads as the just recoil of a life spent in enmity against God's chosen. The trembling of Saul before the shooters is at once the plain fear of a doomed soldier and the shudder of a man who has outrun the mercy he despised, so that even the wound has a voice, and the arrow that finds him speaks of a justice he had long provoked.
4:
Face to face with capture, Saul turns to his armour-bearer with a last, desperate command, and the witnesses read the moment from two sides. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets the scene beside an older suicide — Abimelech, who in Judges 9:54 begged his young armour-bearer to run him through, that it might not be said a woman slew him. Saul's motive is the same dread of dishonour: lest these uncircumcised come and abuse me, he says, preferring death by a friendly stroke to the thrusts of insulting foemen. Ellicott lingers over that word abuse. It means, strictly, to make a toy of, to sport with — the same verb God uses of Egypt, how I have made a toy of Egypt, in Exodus 10:2, and the word for the mockery of a fallen foe in Jeremiah 38:19. What Saul cannot bear is to become the plaything of the Philistines. And when his armour-bearer refuses, he took a sword — literally the sword, his own — and fell upon it. Ellicott notes too that the parallel in 1 Samuel 31:4 adds the words and thrust me through, whether by an inadvertent repetition there or an omission here.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry finds in the same command a stroke of poetic justice. Saul had once commanded Doeg to slay the priests of the LORD; now, in his despair, he commands his own armour-bearer to draw the sword and thrust him through. And in the refusal and its sequel Henry sees the sentence completed: he that was the murderer of the priests is justly left to himself, to become his own murderer, his blood upon his own head.

The two readings meet without friction. Ellicott shows us a proud king recoiling from mockery and choosing the sword; Henry shows us a guilty king overtaken at last by the very violence he had once loosed upon the innocent. The hand that had signed the death-warrant of Nob now grips the hilt turned against its own breast. Whether we look with the grammarian at the ancient horror of falling alive into the uncircumcised captor's hands, or with the preacher at the long arithmetic of divine justice, the verse shows a man who has run out of every refuge but the blade, and who dies as he had made others die — by the sword, and by his own choosing. The first king of Israel ends not in the field's honest clash but on the point of his own weapon, a suicide bred of pride and despair together.
5:
The armour-bearer's death follows his master's in a single stroke, and the witnesses, few here, attend to both the words and the man. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott marks the small textual differences between this account and its parallel. He fell likewise on the sword — where the older narrative of 1 Samuel 31:5 reads his sword, meaning the armour-bearer's own weapon rather than Saul's — and died; and Samuel there adds the touching phrase with him, which the Chronicler seems to drop for brevity, a brevity Ellicott suspects lies behind several of his other small omissions. As for the man's motive, Ellicott judges it a mingling of loyalty to his chief and, perhaps, dread of falling alive into the hands of the same foe Saul had feared: he will not survive the king he served, nor face the Philistines who are already stripping the slain.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sets the incident within his pattern of exact recompense. Saul had disobeyed the plain command of God in refusing to destroy the Amalekites; and now, in a grim echo, his armour-bearer disobeys him — declining the order to destroy his master — so that the disobedience Saul had shown to heaven is answered by disobedience shown to Saul. Even the one man left at his side will not do his bidding at the end.

Between them the witnesses render the loneliness of the scene. A king dies by his own hand for fear of mockery; his last servant, whether from devotion or terror, will not raise a hand against him but turns the blade upon himself and dies beside him. There is fidelity here, of a broken kind — the servant follows his lord even into death — yet it is fidelity spent upon a lost cause, on a mountain the LORD has given over to the enemy. Ellicott is content to name the man's mingled motives and let them stand; Henry alone presses the incident into his larger scheme of recompense, where every stroke of Saul's fall answers some stroke of his sin. The two readings, the textual and the moral, do not quarrel but complete each other, the one attending to what the words say and the other to what the death means. Together they leave the reader with two swords, two suicides, and the silence that falls when the house of Saul has emptied itself of life upon Gilboa, clearing, though none there knew it, the throne for David.
6:
So Saul died, and his three sons, and all his house died together — and the sweeping phrase all his house draws the witnesses to say carefully what it does and does not mean. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke takes it of the family in the field: every branch of Saul's house that had followed him to the war was cut off, the three sons named as the chief among them, and no doubt his officers slain with them. The other wesleyan-arminian voice, John Wesley, is more precise still: all his house means all his children then present with him, that is, his three sons; for Ishbosheth and Mephibosheth were not slain in the battle and lived on. The expression is not to be pressed as though the whole line perished on Gilboa.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott confirms the point from the parallel text and warns against over-reading. Where Chronicles says all his house, the older narrative of 1 Samuel 31:6 reads and his armour-bearer, also all his men, on that day together; the Septuagint adds on that day here and omits all his men there, tending to smooth the differences away. It is mere pedantry, Ellicott says, to press the phrases all his men and all his house against each other; the strength of both expressions is simply meant to convey the completeness of the overthrow. And he reminds the reader that the Chronicler himself well knew that some of Saul's house were not in the battle, since he had just given their genealogy in 1 Chronicles 9:35; what he means is that the chief warriors of the household and the king's immediate following died with him.

The witnesses do not divide. Wesleyan and grammatical-historical alike read the verse as a statement of ruin, not of extinction: the crown, the heir, and the fighting strength of the house of Saul all fell in one day, though a remnant survived to trouble David's early years. The force of the sentence lies in that word together — king and sons and household going down as one, the whole standing order of Saul's kingdom collapsing at a stroke. Yet the very qualification the witnesses insist upon carries its own weight, for the son who was absent, Ishbosheth, and the crippled grandson, Mephibosheth, will keep the story of Saul's house open a little longer, until the LORD's transfer of the kingdom to David, announced at the chapter's close, is fully worked out.
7:
The rout spreads outward from the mountain to the towns, and the witnesses trace both the geography and the grief of it. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott corrects the rendering: the men of Israel that were in the valley is rather those in the plain — the plain of Jezreel where the main battle was fought — and he compares the curt phrasing with the list of returned inhabitants in 1 Chronicles 9:2. The older account in 1 Samuel 31:7 speaks more fully of those on the other side of the plain, and on the other side of Jordan. Ellicott takes the phrase of the people of the surrounding districts: when they saw that the army of Israel had fled and been routed, and that Saul and his sons were dead, they deserted their own cities, which the Philistines then came and occupied. He notes even a nicety of grammar — that the pronoun dwelt in them is here masculine, where Samuel's feminine form is the correct one.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry and the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley read the abandoned towns as the wages of a king's sin, paid by the people. It was a bad time with Israel, Henry says, when they fled before the Philistines and quitted their cities, and the enemy came and dwelt in them; and the lesson stands plain — princes sin, and the people suffer for it. Wesley echoes it almost word for word: princes sin and the people bear the loss; no doubt there was enough in the people themselves to deserve the judgment, yet what divine justice chiefly regarded was the sin of Saul, so that great men above all must beware of kindling God's wrath, since they know not how many may be consumed by that fire for their sakes.

Between the grammarian and the preachers the verse gains its full weight. Ellicott shows the mechanics of a collapse — a routed field, panicked townsfolk, and an enemy pouring into emptied streets; Henry and Wesley show the moral engine beneath it, a nation losing its homes because its king had lost his God. The Philistines who had been confined to their own narrow corner now dwell in Israelite cities as far as the Jordan, and the reader is meant to feel how far the ruin of one disobedient man can reach, spreading from a single body on Gilboa to whole towns given over to the uncircumcised. The suffering of the many is real, and yet the eye of the text, the witnesses agree, is fixed on the guilt of the one.
8:
Morning comes to the battlefield, and the Philistines return to plunder the dead. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott has little to add to the bare fact except a collation: where Chronicles says the Philistines found Saul and his sons fallen in Gilboa, the parallel in 1 Samuel 31:8 reads his three sons; otherwise, he notes, the two verses stand word for word the same. The Chronicler is following his source closely, altering only in the smallest details.

It is the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry who draws the lesson out of the grim discovery. From the triumph of the Philistines over the body of Saul, he says, we may learn that the greater the dignity men are advanced to, the greater the disgrace they stand in danger of falling into. Saul's dead body, precisely because he had been a king, was abused more than any other of the slain that lay upon the mountain; the crown that had exalted him made his corpse the choicest prize for the enemy's malice. Advancement, Henry observes, makes a man a mark: the higher he is lifted, the more conspicuous a target he becomes for the spite of his foes, and what is done to the meanest soldier is nothing to what will be done to a fallen prince.

So the verse that merely records the strippers coming to the slain becomes, in Henry's hands, a meditation on the vanity of earthly greatness. The man who had stood head and shoulders above all Israel now lies stripped among the common dead, his royalty serving only to sharpen his shame. The witnesses do not divide; Ellicott's quiet insistence that the record is faithful and Henry's reflection on the peril of high place lean the same way, toward the sobering truth that no height of rank can shield a man from the judgments of God, and that the honours which lift a king in life may only expose him the more in death. What the Philistines find on the morrow is not merely a body to be plundered but the visible end of a reign that had set itself against the LORD — the anointed of Israel fallen where the arrows had left him, waiting to be made a trophy in the temples of Dagon.
9:
The victors turn from killing to display, and the witnesses fasten on what the Philistines do with the king's remains. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott refines the translation: rather than when they had stripped him, the sense is they stripped him, and carried off his head, where the parallel in 1 Samuel 31:9 says they cut off his head, and stripped his armour off. The phrase carried off his head, he notes, uses the same Hebrew verb as Pharaoh's grim promise in Genesis 40:19, Pharaoh shall lift thy head from off thee. The head and armour were then sent through the land to carry tidings unto their idols — the verb, Ellicott observes, being one used of tidings good or bad, but especially good, as of the runners in 2 Samuel 18:19-20. Where Chronicles has unto their idols, Samuel reads the house of their idols, though the Greek agrees with the reading here.

Ellicott finds in the gesture a revealing theology. There is a strictly local conception of deity implied in the act, he says: the Philistines behave as though their idols could neither see nor hear anything beyond the walls of their own temples, and so must have the news of the victory carried to them, like the Syrians who fancied the LORD a god of the hills but not of the valleys in 1 Kings 20:23 and again in v.28 — the very opposite of the God who planted the ear and formed the eye, as the psalmist urges in Psalms 94:9.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry seizes the same point with a sharper edge. They sent tidings to their idols, he says — poor idols, that knew nothing of what had been done a few miles off until the report was brought to them, and could do nothing then either. The contrast is left to sting: the dumb gods of Philistia must be told of a triumph they did not win and could not see, while the living God of Israel, who had ordered the whole battle, needs no messenger. Between them the witnesses turn a boast into an exposure. What the Philistines meant as the glorifying of Dagon and Ashtaroth becomes, in the reading of the grammarian and the preacher alike, a demonstration of how blind and helpless those idols were — a triumph carried on the head of a dead king to gods who could not lift a finger to gain it, and cannot know of it till it is announced.
10:
The trophies come to rest in the shrines, and the witnesses divide their attention between the archaeology of these gods and the rebuke their worship offers Israel. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott unfolds the names. They put his armour in the house of their gods — or god, as the Greek has it — where the older text of 1 Samuel 31:10 reads the house of Ashtaroth, which the Chronicler or his source seems to paraphrase, perhaps from a reluctance to name the idol. Ashtoreth, he reminds us, had a great temple at Ascalon as the Heavenly Aphrodite; she is the queen of heaven whose worship Jeremiah rebukes in Jeremiah 7:18, honoured across the Semitic world — as Ishtar the chief goddess of Assyria, with famous temples at Nineveh and Arbela, as Athtar among the Sabaeans, and as the Ashtar coupled with Chemosh on the Moabite Stone.

Then the head. They fastened his head in the temple of Dagon — literally, Ellicott says, his skull, the word being that from which Golgotha takes its name in Matthew 27:33. Here the accounts differ: Samuel says they fastened his corpse to the wall of Beth-shan, and it is hardly likely, Ellicott judges, that either reading is a mere corruption of the other; rather the Chronicler has passed over the fate of the body and supplied, from an additional source, the fate of the head. The worship of Dagon itself, he adds, is old, the Accadians having honoured that god, as the name Ismi-Dagan, Dagon hears, attests.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley and the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turn from the idols to Israel's own conscience. If we do not give God the glory of our successes, Wesley warns, even the Philistines will rise up in judgment against us and condemn us: shall Dagon have so great a place in their triumphs, and the true and living God be forgotten in ours? Henry sharpens the same thrust — the Philistines set Saul's armour in the house of their gods and hung his head before Dagon, crediting their idol with the victory; how much more should the people of the LORD own his hand in every deliverance he gives them. The witnesses do not contend. Ellicott's learning lays bare the crowded pantheon of Philistia, and the Wesleyan and Puritan preachers press the irony home: heathen who cannot see beyond their temple walls are yet more careful to honour their false gods than Israel often is to honour the true, so that a dead king's skull nailed up in Dagon's house becomes a mirror held to the ingratitude of the living.
11:
The scene shifts across the Jordan to Jabesh-gilead, and the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke pauses to ask what moved its men to their daring errand. For a full account of the principles of heroism and gratitude from which their action sprang, he refers the reader to his notes on the parallel in 1 Samuel 31:11-12, and he promises a further illustration, furnished by a literary friend, at the close of the chapter. The key, for Clarke, is gratitude: these men had greater cause than any others in Israel to honour the remains of their fallen king.

The reason lay far back in Saul's own history. When Nahash the Ammonite had besieged Jabesh-gilead and threatened to thrust out the right eye of every man in it, it was Saul, in the first flush of his kingship, who mustered Israel and delivered the city, as told in 1 Samuel 11. The men of Jabesh had been preserved by him from destruction, and — what to brave men is more galling still — from bearing the lifelong mark of defeat and the loss of any honourable hope of redeeming their disgrace. That debt they had never forgotten. Now, hearing what the Philistines had done to the body of their deliverer, the strong impulse of gratitude prompted them to a like honourable exertion on his behalf.

So the verse, which merely reports that Jabesh-gilead heard of the outrage, carries beneath its surface the whole memory of a rescue repaid. Clarke will not let the men of Jabesh be admired merely as brave; he wants the reader to see the moral spring of their courage, the natural feeling of humanity and honourable respect quickened into action by a remembered mercy. The city that Saul had saved will not leave his corpse to hang upon a heathen wall. In an age when a king's disgrace might be thought to license the desertion of his memory, the gratitude of one small town beyond the Jordan stands out, and the witness marks it as the first stirring of a debt about to be nobly paid — a single point of light in a chapter otherwise heavy with defeat and dishonour.
12:
The men of Jabesh make good their debt, and the witnesses read the rescue with both a grammarian's care and a sense of its ancient nobility. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott follows the Hebrew word by word. All the valiant men is literally every man of valour; the parallel in 1 Samuel 31:12 adds that they marched all the night, and that they took the bodies from the wall of Beth-shan. The word rendered the body, he observes, is a rare Aramaic term found only here in the Old Testament, where Samuel uses the pure Hebrew synonym — a difference, like several others in the verse, that points to the Chronicler's use of another source. Samuel says they came to Jabesh and burnt them there, and over that word burnt Ellicott weighs two possibilities: since the burning of a corpse was ordinarily a degradation reserved for executed criminals, as in Joshua 7:25, Leviticus 20:14, and Leviticus 21:9, and the Jews did not commonly cremate, the phrase may mean they made a burning of costly spices for him, as at the funerals of kings in Jeremiah 34:5 and 2 Chronicles 16:14; or else the mutilated bodies were exceptionally burnt. That the Chronicler then says they buried their bones, not their bodies, seems to Ellicott to confirm that the flesh had been consumed.

Ellicott finishes with the tree and the mourning. They buried the bones under the oak — the terebinth or turpentine tree, where Samuel names a tamarisk, another mark of a distinct source — and fasted seven days in token of grief, as Job's friends sat silent seven days in Job 2:11-13, and as Ezekiel sat stunned among the exiles in Ezekiel 3:15. For the whole temper of the men of Jabesh he sends the reader back once more to their rescue by Saul in 1 Samuel 11.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke sets the deed against the wide custom of the ancient world. The warriors of old, he shows in an illustration he lays before the reader, honoured the remains of their leaders above almost all things; the contests for the body of a fallen chief were often more determined than the struggle for victory itself, quickened by the old religious dread over the fate of the unburied. What Homer sang of the fight for Patroclus and of the rage over Hector, Clarke reads as the very temper in which the Philistines abused Saul and the men of Jabesh redeemed him. The witnesses do not divide: the one lays bare the seams of the text and the marks of mourning, the other lifts the burial into the company of the noblest ancient rites, and together they let a night-march and a lonely grave under a terebinth stand as the last, brave courtesy shown to the house of Saul.
13:
Now the historian turns from the events to their cause, pronouncing over Saul's grave the verdict that governs the chapter — So Saul died for his transgression which he committed against the LORD, even against the word of the LORD, which he kept not. The reformed John Gill reads it as the plain accounting of providence: this violent and dishonourable death was suffered on account of the sins Saul had been guilty of, his end no chance of battle but a judgment. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke sets beside it the fuller paraphrase of the Targum, which he reports at length: Saul died for the transgression by which he sinned against the Word of the LORD, and because he did not keep the commandment when he warred against Amalek, and because he consulted diviners and sought oracles of them, neither asking counsel of the LORD by Urim and Thummim — for he had slain the priests that were in Nob — therefore the LORD slew him and transferred the kingdom to David the son of Jesse.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott analyses the indictment into its two counts. Saul's unfaithfulness was twofold: first, he did not keep the prophetic word of Jehovah — the disobedience at Gilgal and over Amalek recorded in 1 Samuel 13:13 and 1 Samuel 15:11 — and second, he consulted a necromancer, seeking at En-dor the counsel he owed to God, as in 1 Samuel 28. This broke the standing law of Israel, Turn ye not to them that have familiar spirits, laid down in Leviticus 19:31 and echoed by the prophet in Isaiah 8:19. Here, however, the witnesses part company over the priests of Nob. The Targum, and Clarke after it, number Saul's slaughter of those priests among the causes of his ruin; but Gill observes that this charge is not in the text, and Ellicott insists the same — that no allusion is made in this verdict to Saul's cruel slaughter of the priests in 1 Samuel 22:18, nor to his implacable hatred of David. The Chronicler, they hold, names only the two sins he means to name.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley presses the aggravation in each clause: to sin against the word is to sin against God's express command, the great aggravation of any offence; and the familiar spirit was forbidden by the manifest law of Leviticus 19:31. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers all into a chain of hard lessons: the sin of sinners will certainly find them out, for Saul died for his transgression; no man's greatness can exempt him from the judgments of God; disobedience is a killing thing, since Saul died for not keeping the word of the LORD; and consulting with witches fills up the measure of iniquity as fast as anything can. Reformed, Wesleyan, grammatical-historical, and Puritan alike hear in the sentence the epitaph of the first king — a man undone not by the Philistine bow but by a settled unfaithfulness to his covenant God, who kept neither the word he was given nor the God who gave it.
14:
The verdict reaches its sharpest word — And inquired not of the LORD; therefore he slew him, and turned the kingdom unto David the son of Jesse — and the witnesses face at once the difficulty on its surface. For the earlier history says plainly that Saul did enquire of the LORD, and the LORD answered him not, in 1 Samuel 28:6. How then can it be written that he inquired not? The reformed John Gill answers that Saul's inquiry was only external, careless, and hypocritical, made neither seriously nor sincerely nor with any constancy, so that it was accounted as if he had never inquired at all. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley says the same in his own cadence: Saul did enquire in some sort, but not humbly and penitently, not diligently and importunately, not patiently and perseveringly, nor until he was driven to the last extremity — and by then it was too late.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott traces the failure to the king's temper. Saul had indeed enquired before ever he went down to the witch of En-dor; but at the silence of God his native impatience — the same that had betrayed him at Gilgal in 1 Samuel 13:13 — betrayed him again, and he despaired at once of help from his God instead of seeking it with self-humiliation and penitence. The sin that ruined the first king, Ellicott observes, was essentially the sin that would one day ruin the whole nation, unfaithfulness to the covenant-God, the very word that links v.13 with the like verdicts of 1 Chronicles 5:25 and 1 Chronicles 9:1. On therefore he slew him he insists that God acts through the instrumentality of his creatures: here he employed the Philistines and the suicidal hand of Saul himself, as later he made the Assyrian the rod of guilty peoples in Isaiah 10:5-15 and raised up Cyrus to fulfil his pleasure in Isaiah 44:28. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch concur in the plain sense — because Saul inquired not of the LORD, therefore God slew him — and read the whole chapter as the deliberate threshold of David's reign.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry holds the mystery of that he slew him without flinching: Saul slew himself, and yet it is said that God slew him, for what is done by wicked hands is nonetheless done by the determinate counsel and foreknowledge of God; those who abandon themselves to the devil, as Saul did at En-dor, shall be abandoned to him, and such shall their doom be. So the chapter ends where the Chronicler aimed it from the first: the kingdom is turned unto David the son of Jesse, brought to him by the warriors of Israel who gathered to him in 1 Chronicles 12:23. The witnesses close in one accord — the death that wore the face of a battlefield accident was the judgment of God, and that judgment upon an unfaithful king was, in the very same stroke, the opening of the throne to the man after God's own heart.
